costs: Transition costs and potential short-term funding disruption for institutions currently reliant on diagnosis-linked multipliers
Risk of underfunding actual support needs if census-based allocation is not calibrated to real regional demand
content.equity_impact: Could reduce a documented channel of incentive-driven over-identification, particularly relevant to reports that SNI status increasingly functions as exclusionary in admissions where institutional financing fails to support extra resources; but census-based allocation risks underserving genuinely high-need regions unless carefully calibrated to existing county-level disparities
expected_benefits: Reduced fiscal incentive for schools facing enrollment decline to classify students as SNI to offset revenue loss, addressing a documented theoretical risk
Alignment with international precedent (Finland's 2010 shift to census-based allocation coinciding with reduced full-time special placement)
content.goal: Reduce identification-linked fiscal incentives by redesigning per-pupil funding multipliers and normative financing so that support resources are not contingent on formal diagnostic classification

implementation_steps.action: Commission an incentive-impact analysis of current per-pupil multiplier and task-based financing structure
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Finance and Ministry of Education

implementation_steps.action: Draft amendments transitioning a defined share of SNI-linked funding toward census/needs-based allocation, piloted in selected counties
Establish conditionality audits linking supplementary grants to verified service delivery outcomes

mechanisms: Replacement or supplementation of diagnosis-triggered per-pupil multipliers with census-based or population-level allocation formulas, following the evidenced pattern that census-based systems remove fiscal incentives to over-identify while non-census systems correlate with rising identification

Introduction of stricter accountability requirements tying supplementary SNI grants to documented local service delivery rather than headcount alone, addressing the finding that weak conditionality currently limits the grants' effectiveness
risks: No causal evidence currently links Hungary's specific financing mechanism to the documented identification rise, so the intervention rests on a plausible but untested incentive theory

Poland's contrasting experience shows disability-weighted per-pupil funding was expanded rather than removed, with subventions rising independent of prevalence, suggesting funding redesign choices are not universally convergent
content.title: Shift SEN finance architecture from diagnosis-gated to needs/census-based allocation
